citation_sentences: higher temperatures. This observation corroborates the XRD results (Fig. 2). Though Ni addition facilitates precipitation of B2 phase, it was more pronounced at lower Mn levels i.e., in 15Mn5Ni alloy during heat treatment at 973 K. It is also interesting to notice prominent coarser precipitate (B2) rich zone around deformed austenite in 15Mn5Ni alloy, whereas in 28Mn5Ni alloy fine secondary B2 was precipitated mostly along grain boundaries. In addition, the inset in Fig. 4f shows the colored IPF map for B2 phase alone, and it illustrates the presence of both prior B2 phase and new B2 precipitates formed upon heat treatment at 1173 K. The prior B2 phase grains, which deformed heavily along with γ-matrix in cold rolling, were fully recovered and developed bambootype grain morphology upon heat treatment. Interestingly, the new B2 precipitates showed single crystal type orientations, but displayed some orientation relationship with the adjacent γ-grains.

Dissolution rate of finer B2 particles was seen to be higher than that of B2 stringers. The recrystallization fraction was calculated based on KAM maps as well as GOS <2◦ criteria. Fig. 5 illustrates the evolution of microstructural features (quantified using the OIM-EBSD data) such as recrystallization fraction of austenite grains (Fig. 5a), the mean grain size of recrystallized austenite grains (Fig. 5b), twin fraction (Fig. 5c)

higher temperatures. This observation corroborates the XRD results (Fig. 2). Though Ni addition facilitates precipitation of B2 phase, it was more pronounced at lower Mn levels i.e., in 15Mn5Ni alloy during heat treatment at 973 K. It is also interesting to notice prominent coarser precipitate (B2) rich zone around deformed austenite in 15Mn5Ni alloy, whereas in 28Mn5Ni alloy fine secondary B2 was precipitated mostly along grain boundaries. In addition, the inset in Fig. 4f shows the colored IPF map for B2 phase alone, and it illustrates the presence of both prior B2 phase and new B2 precipitates formed upon heat treatment at 1173 K. The prior B2 phase grains, which deformed heavily along with γ-matrix in cold rolling, were fully recovered and developed bambootype grain morphology upon heat treatment. Interestingly, the new B2 precipitates showed single crystal type orientations, but displayed some orientation relationship with the adjacent γ-grains.

In order to characterize the deformed austenite regions and finer microstructural features such as κ-carbides and B2 particles which were not revealed at SEM-EBSD scale, TEM studies were carried out on selected heat-treated samples. Fig. 6a–d, e-f and g-h show the TEM images of 28Mn0Ni, 28Mn5Ni and 15Mn5Ni alloy samples heat treated at 973 K. Fig. 6b,d,f,h are dark-field TEM micrographs and the rest are bright-field images. The dark-field images were captured from (010) satellite spots corresponding to κ-carbide in selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns shown as insets in Fig. 6a–d. As noticed in EBSD examination (Fig. 4), there were mixed regions of recrystallized and deformed austenite grains in all the alloy samples heat treated at 973 K. In recrystallized austenite regions, fine uniformly distributed intragranular κ-carbides were seen (shown by dotted (orange) arrow in Fig. 6b), while the deformed austenite region contained inhomogeneous and relatively coarse κ-carbides of size 10–60 nm (Fig. 6d). The size of κ-carbides in deformed austenite regions was found to be higher in 15Mn5Ni alloy than that compared to in 28Mn0Ni and 28Mn5Ni alloys under similar conditions. The observations of coarser and higher volume fraction of κ-carbides in deformed austenite and finer ones in recrystallized region are consistent with earlier studies shown in Ni-free LDS [17–19]. In addition to κ-carbides, intergranular B2 particles were observed in 28Mn0Ni alloy samples heat treated at 973 K. One such region marking presence of intergranular B2 particles identified by solid (white) arrow is shown in Fig. 6a. Similarly, fine intragranular B2 (5–20 nm sizes) marked by broken (yellow) arrow, intergranular B2 (50–200 nm sizes) particles as well as κ-carbides were noticed in both 28Mn5Ni (Fig. 6e and f) and 15Mn5Ni (Fig. 6g and h) alloy samples heat-treated at 973 K. The microstructure evolution in all the three alloys under heat treatment at higher temperature of 1173 K is shown in Fig. 7. It is noted that no secondary phase precipitates were seen in 28Mn0Ni alloy (Fig. 7a and b). In case of Ni containing steels heat-treated at 1173 K, fraction of intragranular B2 precipitation decreased with increase in recrystallized austenite fraction (Fig. 7c–f). This indicates that highly strained regions are preferential nucleation sites for these precipitates. Furthermore, the composition profile of B2 was taken using TEM-EDS and is provided in Table 2. This elemental analysis indicated B2 as FeAl based phase but contains significant amounts of Mn and Ni in it, whose content varied with their contents in the steel. Furthermore, the B2 phase enriched with Ni content with increasing the heat treatment temperature.

It has been well established that heat treatment in the two-phase region controls grain growth in most metallic systems because of reduced grain boundary migration and mobility due to decreased diffusivities. This is the case in the present study as well. For example, as can be noted in Fig. 4b $^ { , \mathrm { d } , \mathrm { f } }$ and $\mathrm { F i g }$ . 5b that when the alloys were heat treated at 1173 K significant austenite grain growth was noticed in 28Mn0Ni alloy (B2-free alloy), but not in the other two alloys containing secondary B2 phase. This clearly suggests that heat treatment in the twophase region helps in attaining refined grain structure in LDS alloys as well.

citation_sentences: shown in Fig. 9c. In addition, the effects of heat treatment and composition on strain hardening ability of LDS were evaluated from plastic deformation region of their respective true stress-true strain data in the form of ln(σ) versus ln(ε), and compared in Fig. 9d. All the tensile flow curves showed typical two slope characteristics with strain hardening exponent ‘n’ value quite small (<0.1) at low strain regime. However, the n value at larger strain was found to be significant and varied with the composition of the steel and the heat treatment temperature. For example, in case of 28Mn0Ni alloy the n value increased from 0.16 to 0.50 with increasing the heat treatment temperature from 973 K to 1173 K (Fig. 9c). Both the Ni-containing alloys (28Mn5Ni and 15Mn5Ni) exhibited nearly identical but relatively lower n value of \~0.30 as compared to the 28Mn0Ni alloy heat treated to same temperature of 1173 K (Fig. 9d). The values of n are reported in Table 3. The analysis indicates higher strain hardening ability of Ni-free (28Mn0Ni) alloy than that of Ni-containing LDS alloys. This observation is consistent with the earlier reports [27–29]. The differences in strain hardening capability could primarily be related to the changes in microstructural constituents such as the recrystallization fraction of austenite, the grain size of the recrystallized grains, κ-carbides and B2 particles in these alloys and their effect on dislocation substructure evolution during deformation and is discussed in-detail later.

paper_title: Effect of cold rolling and subsequent heat treatment on microstructural evolution and mechanical properties of Fe-Mn-Al-C-(Ni) based austenitic low-density steels
citation_sentences: As mentioned in previous section, 15Mn5Ni alloy specimens heat treated at 973 K and 1073 K displayed negligible ductility (<1%), whereas 28Mn0Ni and 28Mn5Ni alloy specimens heat treated to the same conditions showed remarkable tensile elongations. Hence, fractography of all the three alloy tensile specimens heat treated at 1023 K but failed in tension at room temperature is presented in Fig. 10a–c, respectively. The morphology of fracture surface of 15Mn5Ni alloy specimen (Fig. 10c) was distinctive from that of the other two alloy specimens (Fig. 10a and b) and is in accordance with the tensile response of the alloys. Both 28Mn0Ni and 28Mn5Ni alloys exhibited dimple morphology whereas quasi-cleavage fracture was seen in 15Mn5Ni alloy specimen. This indicates that the failure in both 28Mn0Ni and 28Mn5Ni alloys was ductile in nature. Fracture in 15Mn5Ni alloy was seemed to crack initiation-controlled with fast propagation of cracks. Large numbers of microcracks were seen on fracture surface of 15Mn5Ni alloy specimen. It is to be noted that 15Mn5Ni alloy showed much lesser recrystallization of austenite matrix and large volume fraction of secondary phase precipitation along grain boundaries forming precipitate rich zones. Further, cross section of the failed tensile specimen of 15Mn5Ni alloy heat treated at 1023 K was examined in-detail. Fig. 10d shows a scanning electron micrograph of one such crack regions revealing presence of intergranular cracks. Fig. 10e and f shows the OIM-IPF and phase maps, respectively of another cracked region, clearly indicating propagation of crack along γ/B2 interphase boundaries.

caption: Fig. 11. TEM images showing post tensile deformed microstructures of 28Mn0Ni alloy in (a) HR + CR+973 K condition deformed to a true strain, ε\~0.14, (b–e) HR + CR+1173 K condition deformed to ε\~0.02, 0.08 and 0.30. Here, (a) shows dislocation multiplication and cell formation, (b–d) show refinement of slip bands with progress of deformation (strain), and (e) indicates interaction between dislocation and prior annealing twins.

paper_title: Effect of cold rolling and subsequent heat treatment on microstructural evolution and mechanical properties of Fe-Mn-Al-C-(Ni) based austenitic low-density steels
citation_sentences: In addition, microstructures of selected interrupted tensile specimens of 28Mn0Ni and 15Mn5Ni alloys were examined in order to understand the dislocation substructure evolution. Figs. 11 and 12 show the posttensile deformed microstructures of 28Mn0Ni and 15Mn5Ni alloys, respectively. Fig. 11a shows TEM bright-field image of 28Mn0Ni-HR + CR+973 K sample deformed up to a true strain of ε\~0.14. Fig. 11b–e and 12a-d show the TEM bright-field images, respectively of 28Mn0Ni and 15Mn5Ni alloys heat treated at 1173 K, as a function of the applied strain. There was progressive substructure evolution starting from dislocation multiplication leading to planar slip activity followed by slip band refinement with the applied strain in the fully recrystallized and precipitate-free austenite grains of all the alloys. In the deformed austenite regions of partially recrystallized sample (HR + CR+973 K), fine cross slip activity resulting into formation of dislocation cell structure was seen (Fig. 11a). In regions of post-deformed austenite containing coarser and fine κ-carbides there were certain differences in slip band evolution. Dislocations were cutting through fine κ-carbides evolving into slip band structure with a mean slip band spacing of \~200 ± 30 nm. Further, dislocation-twin interaction was observed and is shown in Fig. 11e. In case of the alloys containing coarse and high fraction of κ -carbides along with B2 particles refinement of slip band spacing was delayed at earlier stages of deformation, which resulted in inhomogeneous slip band spacing. Non-shearable B2 particles were also noticed as obstacles to dislocation motion and thereby early initiation of slip band refinement. In summary, inhomogeneous slip band spacing was developed in Ni containing LDS alloys, whereas Ni-free LDS (28Mn0Ni) alloy exhibited homogeneous slip band spacing under similar heat-treated conditions of HR + CR+1173 K.

caption: Fig. 12. TEM images showing post tensile deformed microstructures of 15Mn5Ni alloy in $\mathrm { H R } + \mathrm { C R } { + } 1 1 7 3$ K condition deformed to ε\~0.02, 0.05 and 0.14. Here, (a) indicates dislocation-B2 particle interaction and (b–d) show dislocation multiplication and refinement of slip bands with strain.
